articles.title: Elon Musk's SpaceX valued at nearly $1.8tn ahead of record share sale
articles.description: The public sale is also expected to make Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire.

articles.title: How AI is changing the routine mammogram
articles.description: In a Q&A, Yale radiologist John Lewin explains how artificial intelligence can not only be used to detect breast cancer but also predict future risk.

articles.title: New Antibiotic Found in Dirt Targets Superbugs in a Way ‘Never Been Seen Before’
articles.description: Researchers tested their newly discovered peptide against one common antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Only about one in every hundred million survived to pass on genetic resistance.

articles.title: The U.S. stockpiles oil in huge underground salt caverns. Here’s why
articles.description: Salt, with its ability to seal liquid in, is uniquely suited to storing the nation’s Strategic Petroleum Reserve

articles.title: Is It a Super El Niño Year? It Could Turn the World’s Weather Upside Down
articles.description: From a wet winter in the Southwest to fewer Atlantic hurricanes, this is what to expect as a potential super El Niño takes shape.

articles.title: Collapsing stars could spawn mini-universes, offering new path to gravastars
articles.description: Stars shine because atoms fuse in their interiors, releasing energy. When a very massive star has exhausted its nuclear fuel, radiation pressure can no longer provide sufficient counterforce to gravity. The star then collapses under its own mass until only a single point remains: the singularity.

articles.title: Meet LEV-2, a baseball-sized and absurdly cute moon robot
articles.description: This tiny robot might look like a high-tech hamster ball, but it could hasten lunar exploration

articles.title: Did Life Start When Impacts Created Vast Hydrothermal Systems in Earth's Crust?
articles.description: Earth was bombarded by impactors in its first couple billion years. These impacts created a vast network of hydrothermal systems in the crust that could've spawned life. New research examines their extent.

articles.title: When it comes to predicting people’s preferences, it pays to consider “the power of three”
articles.description: MIT researchers provide a major upgrade to the nearly century-old idea of random utility models.

articles.title: Chemists snap together complex 3D molecules from highly reactive 'radicals'—without losing their shape
articles.description: Building the complex 3D molecules needed for new medicines has always been a bit like assembling a puzzle with pieces that keep trying to flip over. Now, chemists at Scripps Research have found a way to snap two such molecular pieces together while keeping their original 3D shapes intact, even when using some of the most reactive molecules in chemistry: free radicals.

articles.title: Silent Impact: What If Routine Hits Are Football’s Biggest Threat to the Brain?
articles.description: By partnering with athletes and leveraging cutting-edge neuroscience, Brad Mahon is helping to rewrite the playbook for the future of football safety.

articles.title: STAT+: Novartis doubles down on molecular glue strategy
articles.description: Seniors' drug costs rise sharply, Enliven's leukemia drugs, and more biotech news from The Readout

articles.title: Dietitians reveal: This is the best way to eat a sweet potato to preserve its vitamins
articles.description: Sweet potatoes are considered one of the healthiest foods, exceptionally rich in beta-carotene. Experts explain which cooking methods better preserve their nutritional value.

articles.title: Palantir, World’s Weepiest Eye of Sauron, Sues Mayor of London After Losing a Contract
articles.description: "The Met only fully engaged with one potential supplier: Palantir." The post Palantir, World’s Weepiest Eye of Sauron, Sues Mayor of London After Losing a Contract appeared first on Futurism .

articles.title: Scientists discover collagen, the human body's most abundant protein, is liquid-like inside cells
articles.description: Collagen, the protein that builds skin, bones, tendons and organs, exists inside cells as a liquidlike droplet rather than the long, rigid rod seen in textbooks over the last half-century, according to a new study from the Center for Genomic Regulation (CRG) in Barcelona.

articles.title: Wary investors hit by a natural disaster seek premium on equity investment
articles.description: Investor caution soon after experiencing a natural disaster increases the cost of capital for businesses hoping to grow, new academic research suggests.

articles.title: Surveyors say subdued housing market may be starting to stabilise
articles.description: The Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors said it is too early to interpret the research as the start of a recovery

articles.title: Q&A: Expert offers insight on stopping the New World screwworm
articles.description: NC State University entomologist Maxwell Scott is among a handful of people worldwide with the most thorough understanding of the genetics and life cycle of the New World screwworm, a blowfly that lays its eggs in wounds or vulnerable spots in livestock, pets, wildlife and humans and feeds on their living tissue.

articles.title: CATalyst Council Reports After Five Years of Declining Veterinary Visits, a Demographic Forecast Resets Industry Expectations
articles.description: NORTH CANTON, Ohio--(BUSINESS WIRE)--The CATalyst Council today released “Puppocalypse, Kitten Craze, and the Expectations Reset: What Pet Demographics Reveal About U.S. Veterinary Visits Through 2035,” a white paper that explains why the U.S. veterinary services industry is now in its fifth consecutive year of declining clinical visits and why that decline is likely to persist through the next decade. The analysis was co-authored by CATalyst Council, Vetsource, Kynetec, and Dedekind Cut Labs a

articles.title: China’s Tianwen-2 spacecraft arrives at one of Earth’s mysterious ‘quasi-moons’
articles.description: The Tianwen-2 spacecraft is slowly closing in on the near-Earth asteroid Kamo‘oalewa, on a mission that would bring China’s first asteroid samples back to Earth in 2027

articles.title: Humans prefer to walk anticlockwise - and scientists have no idea why
articles.description: You walk into an art gallery, museum, or shopping centre - which way do you turn? According to a new study, the answer is probably left.

articles.title: People Living Near xAI’s Dirty Data Centers Are Furious About the SpaceX IPO
articles.description: Elon Musk is set to make hundreds of billions even as communities in Mississippi and Tennessee are fighting to stop the gas turbines powering xAI's supercomputers.

articles.title: A Newer Approach to Editing Embryos Ignites Debate
articles.description: Fertility specialists, biotech companies and ethicists are divided over whether progress in early gene editing would wipe out diseases or trigger a rush toward enhancement.

articles.title: Millions of homes in London, Essex and Kent are at risk of SINKING, scientists warn - as they call for urgent mitigation measures
articles.description: Millions of homes in London, Essex, and Kent are at risk of sinking into the ground, scientists have warned.

articles.title: Sitting all day in a closed office? This is the worrying thing happening to your facial skin
articles.description: You do not give it importance, but prolonged sitting in the office has a decisive effect not only on the body, but also on the face. Meet the concept: "Digital acne".

articles.title: Democrats Once Vowed to Stop Oil and Gas. Now They’re Not So Sure.
articles.description: As the midterm elections approach, many leading Democrats are rethinking their approach to climate change.

articles.title: After seeing Parkinson's take his father, this Googler found a new mission: teaching AI to chase cures
articles.description: Vivek Natarajan is using AI to help doctors and scientists chase new cures, inspired by his father's battle with Parkinson's disease.

articles.title: I Was Scammed Buying GLP-1s Online. I’m Not Alone
articles.description: Customers have complained that a telehealth network selling compounded GLP-1s has been ripping them off—even after it had to pay $5 million to clients as part of a settlement with the US government.

articles.title: From neon mosquitoes to winged migrations, top images captured by scientists
articles.description: Scientists use photography to captures moments of wonder amid the scientific process.

articles.title: This is the healthiest snack for the World Cup
articles.description: Try the snack that provides protein, dietary fiber, and long–lasting satiety, and is also very easy to make at home.

articles.description: La fuerte sacudida sufrida por los semiconductores sigue golpeando a Estados Unidos. Los resultados publicados anoche por Oracle reavivaron las dudas sobre la rentabilidad de la fiebre inversora en...

articles.title: The world's first anti-ageing gene therapy has just been injected into a human - here's what it does
articles.description: World’s first anti-ageing gene therapy trial begins as ER-100 is injected into a human eye, testing partial cellular reprogramming to restore vision and fight ageing

articles.title: Scientists debunk common health misconception about living with cats
articles.description: Cat allergen exposure is common even outside home, scientists say

articles.title: Is this egg healthier than regular eggs?
articles.description: They are small, highly photogenic, and considered more prestigious, but are quail eggs healthier than regular eggs? Here are all the facts.

articles.title: Could AI transform mental health research? New study tests whether LLMs can simulate human emotions
articles.description: AI language models can mimic human emotional states, opening new doors for mental health research, according to a new study.

articles.title: How can we improve noisy labor market data?
articles.description: Data on labor markets is incredibly important to policymakers, from state and federal legislators to the central bank and Treasury Department. But this data is often noisy or incomplete. A new BPEA paper draws on a large body of data sets going back to 1960 to develop a novel approach to studying labor markets that can help fill these gaps. On this episode of the Brookings Podcast on Economic Activity, Brookings Senior Fellow Louise Sheiner talks to Ayşegül Şahin, one of the paper authors, about this new methodology.

articles.title: Private space tourism is taking off – but laws on outer space are from another era
articles.description: Commercial operators are launching more rockets and carrying more passengers than ever before. But international space law was written in the 1960s.

articles.title: Smacking leads to worse exam results, research finds
articles.description: Smacking children can lead to future behavioural problems and worse exam results, according to new research.

articles.title: Scientists investigate origins of a ‘ghost particle’ 30x more powerful than any other
articles.description: Three years ago, a single “ghost particle” soared into the Mediterranean with more energy than any ever observed before.Category: Astronomy, ...

articles.title: Where humpbacks gather near Tokyo's remote islands could reshape whale watching and conservation
articles.description: Humpback whales are one of the most popular species for whale watching. Since they are active close to the water's surface, groups of whale watchers can often see them breaching and diving during breeding periods. One popular whale watching destination is the Ogasawara Islands—also known as the Bonin Islands—a remote archipelago about 1,000 kilometers south of Tokyo.

articles.title: Study suggests Dante's Hell was inspired by an asteroid impact
articles.description: New research is offering a dramatic new way to read Dante Alighieri’s Inferno: not simply as a religious vision of Hell, but as an early attempt to ...

articles.title: Annual global migration has nearly tripled since 2000, reshaping where and how people move
articles.description: Global migration has risen sharply from approximately 13 million people per year in 2000 to around 35 million people per year in 2023. This is according to a new dataset on human migration published in Nature by researchers from the London School of Economics and Political Science (LSE), IIASA and the University of Hong Kong.

articles.title: NASA's CloudCube pioneers miniaturized radar to study clouds, precipitation
articles.description: A compact, multifrequency radar built by a team at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory will make it easier to collect information about dynamic cloud systems. Called CloudCube, this new instrument simultaneously probes the atmosphere with three radar signals, spanning 36 to 240 GHz, for optimized sensitivity to a wide range of water droplet and ice particle sizes.

articles.title: Data Shows Fundamental Analysis Drives Superior Investment Returns Across Market Cycles
articles.description: While financial markets continue to evolve with algorithmic trading and artificial intelligence, one investment approach has consistently demonstrated its value across decades of market cycles: fundamental analysis. This time-tested methodology, which focuses on evaluating a company’s intrinsic value through financial statements, business fundamentals, and economic conditions, remains a cornerstone of successful investing strategies. The compelling... The post Data Shows Fundamental Analysis Drives Superior Investment Returns Across Market Cycles appeared first on Equity Insider .

articles.title: Lab-aged moon rock offers new clues about how water forms on the lunar surface
articles.description: Georgia Tech researchers recreated solar wind effects to reveal how the moon's surface changes over time.

articles.title: Wheat root compounds suppress soil microbes, helping retain nitrogen and cut emissions
articles.description: On a quiet weekend morning in a greenhouse in Aarhus University Flakkebjerg, rows of wheat plants stand with their roots submerged in clear water. There is no soil, no buffer, no automation to take over the work. Every day, weekends and holidays included, postdoctoral researcher Purna Kumar Khatri comes by to check them. He adjusts the pH drop by drop. If he does not, the roots will suffer. And the experiment will fail.

articles.title: Meeting the Grand Challenges of Neural Engineering, Together
articles.description: The inaugural neural engineering symposium highlights Duke's collaborative vision for neurotechnology The post Meeting the Grand Challenges of Neural Engineering, Together appeared first on Duke Biomedical Engineering .

articles.title: Gorillas can learn to trust humans even after years of poaching pressure, research shows
articles.description: A new study led by Concordia researchers suggests that carefully habituating wild gorillas to nonthreatening humans can help the animals overcome long-term aversions to people in areas with a history of human-led disturbances such as poaching.

articles.title: Basketball can make you better at math
articles.description: Combining math concepts with sports can help boost your fractions game. The post Basketball can make you better at math appeared first on Popular Science .

articles.title: UC Irvine launches nation’s first multischool depression research institute
articles.description: Event brings together leading scholars, clinicians and community leaders to accelerate breakthroughs in prevention and treatment
